21.dangerous_reason: Unencrypted file transfer. Credentials are sent in plaintext and can be sniffed on the network. Prefer SFTP or SCP instead.

steps: Find 'Microsoft FTP Service' or 'FTP Publishing Service'
Optional: Open 'Turn Windows features on or off' → uncheck IIS → FTP Server
If using proftpd: sudo systemctl stop proftpd && sudo systemctl disable proftpd
Verify: sudo ss -tlnp | grep :21 (should return nothing)
Use SFTP over SSH instead: sftp user@pi-ip

22.dangerous_reason: Remote shell access. Brute-force and credential-stuffing attacks are constant. Weak passwords or exposed keys allow full device control.

steps: Verify: sudo systemctl status ssh (should show 'inactive')
Or: sudo raspi-config → Interface Options → SSH → Disable

23.dangerous_reason: Unencrypted remote shell. Completely obsolete and replaced by SSH. Session hijacking and credential theft are trivial on local networks.

steps: Uncheck 'Telnet Client' and 'Telnet Server' if listed
Find 'Telnet' service → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Use SSH with key-based login instead

25.dangerous_reason: Mail server port. If this is not your intentional mail server, it is an open-relay risk and a common spam and phishing vector.

steps: Find 'Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (SMTP)' or IIS mail services
Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
In IIS Manager, remove any SMTP virtual server if present
If using sendmail: sudo systemctl stop sendmail && sudo systemctl disable sendmail
Home Pis rarely need a mail server — disable unless intentional

53.dangerous_reason: DNS server port. If you are not running a DNS server, this should not be open. Misconfigured DNS is abused in amplification DDoS attacks (CVE-2023-28458 class issues on various DNS software).

steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Or: Server Manager → Remove DNS Server role if installed
If using dnsmasq on Pi as LAN DNS only, bind to local interface — not 0.0.0.0

pi.description: Stop public DNS on Raspberry Pi
steps: Run: sudo systemctl stop bind9 (if installed)
Pi-hole/dnsmasq should listen on LAN IP only, never be port-forwarded
Do not expose port 53 to the internet

80.dangerous_reason: Unencrypted web traffic. Logins and session cookies can be intercepted. If you run a web server, use HTTPS (443) with valid TLS instead.

windows.description: Disable HTTP web server on Windows
steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Or: Turn Windows features off → uncheck 'Internet Information Services'

linux.description: Stop HTTP web server on Linux
steps: Run: sudo systemctl stop apache2 (or nginx)
Run: sudo systemctl disable apache2 (or nginx)
If web server is needed, configure HTTPS on port 443 only
Run: sudo systemctl stop apache2 (or nginx/lighttpd)
Use HTTPS or restrict to localhost for admin panels
Do not port-forward port 80 from your router

139.dangerous_reason: Legacy Windows file sharing. Highly exploitable — used by worms like Conficker and EternalBlue-era lateral movement on flat networks.

steps: Open Settings → Network & Internet → Advanced network settings → More adapter options
Right-click your connection → Properties → IPv4 → Advanced → WINS tab
Disable 'File and Printer Sharing' if not needed
Run: sudo systemctl stop nmbd smbd
Run: sudo systemctl disable nmbd smbd
Disable Samba unless you intentionally share files on the LAN

443.dangerous_reason: Encrypted web server. If unintended, stop the service. If legitimate, restrict with firewall rules and keep software patched (Heartbleed CVE-2014-0160 affected TLS stacks).

windows.description: Manage HTTPS web server on Windows
steps: If IIS is unintended: disable 'World Wide Web Publishing Service'
Press Win+R, type 'wf.msc' → restrict inbound 443 to trusted networks
Keep Windows and IIS fully updated
If unintended: sudo systemctl stop nginx apache2
Run: sudo systemctl disable nginx (or apache2)
If needed: bind to specific IPs and use firewall (ufw allow from LAN only)
If unintended web server: sudo systemctl stop nginx apache2
Use ufw to allow 443 only from your LAN subnet
Do not expose admin panels to the internet without authentication

445.dangerous_reason: Primary ransomware vector. WannaCry (MS17-010/EternalBlue) and NotPetya exploited SMB. Should never be exposed beyond a trusted LAN.

steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Settings → Network → disable 'File and Printer Sharing' on public networks
Run: sudo systemctl stop smbd nmbd
Run: sudo systemctl disable smbd nmbd
Verify: sudo systemctl status smbd (should show 'inactive')
Run: sudo systemctl stop smbd nmbd
Run: sudo systemctl disable smbd nmbd

554.dangerous_reason: IP camera and video streaming. If unintended, stop the service — exposed streams leak live video. Default camera credentials are brute-forced constantly.

windows.description: Secure or disable RTSP on Windows
steps: Identify the camera app or NVR using port 554
Change all default admin passwords on cameras
Disable UPnP and remote access in camera settings
Do not port-forward 554 from your router
Stop the service: sudo systemctl stop mediamtx (or your NVR service)
Keep cameras on a separate VLAN or guest network
Stop camera streaming service: sudo systemctl stop <rtsp-service>
Use local viewing only — do not expose Pi cameras to the internet

873.dangerous_reason: File sync daemon. If unintended, stop rsync — misconfigured rsync modules allow unauthenticated or weakly authenticated file access and data exfiltration.

steps: Rsync on Windows usually runs via Cygwin, WSL, or cwRsync
Stop the rsync daemon process or Windows service
Remove rsync from startup if not intentionally used
If using rsync for backups, bind to localhost or require SSH tunnel only

1433.dangerous_reason: Extremely valuable target for ransomware and data theft. Should NEVER be exposed to the internet. SQL injection and brute-force attacks are automated at scale.

steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Or: SQL Server Configuration Manager → disable TCP/IP protocol
Block port 1433 in Windows Firewall for all public networks
Verify: sudo ss -tlnp | grep :1433 (should return nothing)
MSSQL is rarely needed on Pi — use SQLite for local projects

1521.dangerous_reason: Critical enterprise database. Should NEVER be exposed. Contains sensitive business data — targeted by APT groups and ransomware (CVE-2021-44228 Log4j affected Oracle middleware stacks).

steps: Right-click each → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Run: sudo systemctl stop oracle-xe (or oracledb)
Oracle is not typically run on Pi
If a listener is open: sudo ss -tlnp | grep :1521
Stop and disable the unexpected service immediately
Use SQLite or PostgreSQL bound to localhost for Pi projects

1900.dangerous_reason: Universal Plug and Play can automatically open firewall holes. Abused in DDoS amplification attacks and Mirai botnet propagation on IoT devices.

windows.description: Disable UPnP on Windows and router
steps: Log into your router admin page and disable UPnP
Windows Settings → Network → disable 'Network Discovery' on public networks
Do not allow apps to automatically configure port forwarding
Disable UPnP on your router (most important step)
Disable UPnP in your router settings
Do not run UPnP services on Pi unless absolutely required

2049.dangerous_reason: Allows remote file mounts over the network. Misconfigured exports permit unauthenticated access — used in lateral movement and data theft on Linux networks.

steps: Uncheck 'Services for NFS' and 'Client for NFS'
Press Win+R, type 'services.msc' → disable 'Server for NFS' if present
Use SMB or SFTP instead for file sharing
Edit /etc/exports — remove all export lines or comment them out
Use SSH/SFTP for file access instead

3306.dangerous_reason: Relational database with sensitive data. Exposed instances are scanned and brute-forced constantly. GDPR and credential databases are common exfiltration targets.

steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Use SQLite for Pi projects unless MySQL is essential

3389.dangerous_reason: RDP brute-force and credential stuffing are among the top attack types (BlueKeep CVE-2019-0708 affected older systems). Exposed RDP gives full desktop control.

steps: Select 'Don't allow remote connections to this computer'
Settings → System → Remote Desktop → toggle Off
Most Linux systems do not run RDP by default
Pi uses VNC (5900), not RDP, by default
If xrdp installed: sudo systemctl stop xrdp && sudo systemctl disable xrdp
Disable VNC via: sudo raspi-config → Interface Options → VNC → Disable

5432.dangerous_reason: PostgreSQL database. Should NEVER be exposed to the internet. Automated scans exploit weak passwords and misconfigured pg_hba.conf for full data access.

steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Bind to localhost only if your project requires PostgreSQL

5900.dangerous_reason: Remote desktop without adequate encryption by default on many implementations. Exposed VNC allows screen viewing and keyboard control — common brute-force target.

steps: Press Win+R, type 'services.msc' → stop VNC Server service
For RealVNC: sudo systemctl stop vncserver-x11-serviced

5984.dangerous_reason: NoSQL document database. Exposed CouchDB with admin party mode allows all data to be read and written without authentication — mass data breaches occurred in 2017.

steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
If running in Docker: docker stop <couchdb-container>
Edit /opt/couchdb/etc/local.ini — bind to 127.0.0.1 only if needed locally
Never expose CouchDB to the internet without authentication enabled

6379.dangerous_reason: In-memory cache/database with no authentication by default. Complete data exposure and remote code execution via CONFIG command — used to deploy malware at scale.

steps: Set Startup type to Disabled → Stop
If in Docker: docker stop <redis-container> publishing 6379
Bind to localhost only if a local app needs Redis

8080.dangerous_reason: Alternative HTTP port with the same risk as port 80. Admin panels, proxies, and dev servers often use default credentials on this port.

steps: Note the PID → Task Manager → Details → end process
Or disable the service in services.msc (Tomcat, Jenkins, etc.)
Do not port-forward 8080 from your router
Run: sudo systemctl stop <service-name> (tomcat, jenkins, etc.)
Bind dev servers to 127.0.0.1 only during development

pi.description: Stop HTTP alt on Raspberry Pi
steps: Stop the web dashboard or IoT service

8443.dangerous_reason: Alternative HTTPS port. Stop if unintended. If legitimate, restrict with firewall rules and enforce strong authentication on admin interfaces.

steps: Stop the application in services.msc or Task Manager
Press Win+R, type 'wf.msc' → block inbound 8443 on public networks
Change default admin passwords on any device using this port
Use ufw: sudo ufw deny 8443 or allow from LAN only

pi.description: Stop HTTPS alt on Raspberry Pi
steps: Stop and disable the listening service
Use VPN or local access instead of exposing 8443 to the internet

9100.dangerous_reason: Raw printer port. If you do not have a network printer, disable it. Allows print job injection, firmware attacks, and lateral movement on corporate networks.

steps: Settings → Bluetooth & devices → Printers → disable sharing if not needed
Press Win+R, type 'wf.msc' → block port 9100 on public/guest networks
Update printer firmware from the manufacturer website
Do not port-forward 9100 from your router
Edit /etc/cups/cupsd.conf — restrict Listen and Allow to localhost or LAN
Do not expose printer ports to the internet
If Pi shares a printer via CUPS, restrict cupsd.conf to LAN only

9200.dangerous_reason: Search and indexing engine. If exposed, all indexed data — often logs with credentials and PII — is readable. Ransomware groups target open Elasticsearch clusters.

steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Edit elasticsearch.yml: network.host: 127.0.0.1 and enable xpack security
Elasticsearch is heavy for Pi — use local SQLite or bind to localhost only

27017.dangerous_reason: NoSQL database. Older versions had no authentication by default — thousands of databases were wiped in 2017. Full read/write access if exposed without auth.

steps: Right-click → Properties → Startup type: Disabled → Stop
Use SQLite for Pi projects unless MongoDB is essential

50070.dangerous_reason: Hadoop HDFS NameNode web UI. If exposed, attackers can browse and manipulate the entire data lake — used in big-data exfiltration and ransomware campaigns.

steps: Hadoop on Windows is uncommon — check WSL or Docker
Stop Hadoop services or Docker containers publishing 50070
Run stop-all.sh or stop-dfs.sh in Hadoop install directory if present
Disable Hadoop services from starting at boot
Bind NameNode to localhost only in hdfs-site.xml if needed locally
Hadoop is rarely run on Pi in production
Stop the Hadoop or Docker service immediately
Do not expose big-data cluster ports to the internet
